one_line_takeaway: Framing poisoning attacks as a tunable effectiveness-vs-detectability bilevel optimization (with a novel per-batch objective normalization) shows that TRIM, Huber, SEVER, and Proda not only fail to stop stealthy regression poisoning but can make model error worse than deploying no defense at all, motivating BayesClean, a Bayesian-LR-uncertainty-based defense that is more robust once poisoning fractions exceed roughly 20-30%.
key_finding: Existing defenses (TRIM, Huber, SEVER, Proda) largely fail to mitigate the paper's stealthy poisoning attacks, and in many settings the 'Test Defense Gain' is negative -- i.e., deploying the defense produces worse test error than deploying no defense at all -- meaning the defenses can be actively counterproductive, not merely ineffective. This negative-gain effect appears even for alpha=1 (fully aggressive, non-stealthy) attacks in several dataset/defense combinations, and becomes more pronounced as alpha decreases (more stealth) and as the poisoning fraction increases. On DNNs, the same defenses perform even worse (lower test defense gain) than on LR under the identical attack. TRIM in particular is shown, even when it is given the (unrealistic) advantage of knowing the true number of poisoning points, to fail above roughly 24% poisoning because it assumes poisoned points always have larger loss than clean points, an assumption that breaks down once the fitted model has been pulled substantially toward the poison. BayesClean, the paper's own defense, is shown to be more stable across poisoning ratios and to outperform TRIM/SEVER/Proda when the poisoning fraction is large (roughly above 20-30%), including under stealthy (low-alpha) attacks, without needing to pre-specify the poisoning fraction to reject; DUTI (which uses a trusted set) also performs well in general but poorly on Boston Housing. The authors attribute BayesClean's robustness to the fact that a Bayesian LR model's predictive variance increases when poison follows a distribution different from the clean data, even when point-wise error alone no longer discriminates poison from clean points.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is threefold: (1) a novel formulation of data-poisoning attacks against regression models as a multiobjective bilevel optimization problem that trades off attack effectiveness (test MSE/NMSE) against a new soft, model-based 'detectability-risk' function R (defined as the product of two loss terms evaluated against clean-model parameters shifted by +/- one residual standard deviation), combined via a scalarization weight alpha in [0,1]; (2) a normalization method for the two competing objectives (normalizing the detectability term by its alpha=1 maximum, and re-normalizing the effectiveness term per poisoning batch relative to the previous batch's loss) that the authors show is necessary to avoid one objective dominating the gradient-based optimization (Fig. 2 vs Fig. 3); and (3) BayesClean, a new defense built on Bayesian Linear Regression that rejects points based on their location within the model's posterior predictive variance (using thresholds c1 <= c2 to bin points into accepted/flagged-for-inspection/rejected sets), which does not require the defender to pre-specify the fraction of poisoning points to reject and does not require a trusted clean validation set.
threat_model: Data poisoning against regression learners (Linear Regression and feed-forward DNNs). Attacker capability: can inject np poisoning points (full data manipulation: both features and labels) into the training set, up to a significant fraction of it (tested up to 40%); scenario motivated by IoT/OT sensor supply chains where many identical devices can be compromised cheaply. Attacker knowledge: Perfect-Knowledge (PK) of training data, features, model architecture and objective function, but Limited-Knowledge (LK) of the deployed defense -- attacks are crafted against an undefended/standard model and then transferred to evaluate against defended models (not adaptive to a specific defense). Attacker goal: indiscriminate, error-generic availability attack, maximizing test-set MSE while minimizing a detectability-risk objective so that a defender that inspects the training set does not discard it. This is explicitly a worst-case-knowledge-of-data but limited-knowledge-of-defense threat model, and the authors argue it is more realistic than the fully adaptive PK-of-defense threat models common in prior work.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations/caveats: (1) the hypothesis for why error-based defenses fail at high poisoning ratios (clean-point error approaching or surpassing poisoned-point error once the model has shifted) is explicitly flagged as something to 'explore further in future work,' not something they formally prove; (2) BayesClean itself is not uniformly best -- on DNNs (Fig. 11) the negative test-defense-gain values indicate that 'defending DNNs is more challenging' and BayesClean's own advantage is smaller/less consistent there, and the paper states 'further research is needed on methods to defend DNNs' in regression settings; (3) BayesClean is derived from a Bayesian Linear Regression model even when applied to DNN-targeted attacks, which is an approximation, not a Bayesian-DNN-native defense; (4) the two thresholds c1 <= c2 used by BayesClean are fixed constants set in advance and held fixed across poisoning ratios and datasets -- the paper does not present a principled method for choosing them per-dataset; (5) evaluation uses random (not time-aware) train/test splits specifically to avoid confounding attack effectiveness with concept drift, which the authors acknowledge is a simplification relative to deployment settings with drift; (6) the attack solves a strongly NP-hard bilevel optimization via gradient-based local search (RMD/hypergradient ascent), so results reflect local optima, not certified worst-case attacks; (7) the threat model assumes the attacker has full (PK) knowledge of training data/model/objective, which the authors themselves note is a 'worst case' rather than a typical real-world attacker capability, even though the defense-knowledge assumption (LK) is deliberately relaxed toward realism.

datasets_used: Loan (Lending Club peer-to-peer interest-rate regression, preprocessed as in Jagielski et al. 2018)
Heart Disease (preprocessed as in Weerasinghe et al. 2021)
Appliances (energy prediction, preprocessed as in Weerasinghe et al. 2021)
quantitative_results: Test NMSE for the undefended LR model increases with alpha (stealth-effectiveness trade-off parameter) on both Loan and Heart Disease datasets, evaluated at alpha = 1.0, 0.5, 0.3, 0.1 (Fig. 5)

Against LR: for most defenses (TRIM, Huber, SEVER) test defense gain is positive only below roughly 15% poisoning fraction and turns negative (defense worse than no defense) above that, across Loan, Heart Disease, Boston Housing, and Appliances datasets, at alpha = 1.0, 0.3, and 0.1 (Figs. 6-7)

TRIM fails to reject poisoning points above approximately 24% poisoning fraction even when given the unrealistic advantage of knowing the true number of poisoning points in advance (Fig. 9 synthetic comparison)

BayesClean's test defense gain vs. TRIM, SEVER, Proda, and DUTI (trusted-set) is more stable across poisoning fractions 0-40% and outperforms them once poisoning exceeds roughly 20-30%, across all four real-world datasets and alpha = 1.0/0.3/0.1 (Fig. 10)

On DNNs, all baseline defenses (TRIM, Huber, SEVER, Proda) show lower test defense gain than the equivalent LR case, and BayesClean's advantage over baselines persists but with more negative gain overall, indicating DNNs are harder to defend (Figs. 8 and 11)
DUTI performs comparably well to BayesClean in most cases (owing to its trusted validation set) but performs poorly specifically on the Boston Housing dataset
baselines_compared: TRIM (Jagielski et al. 2018) - iterative trimmed-loss training subset selection
SEVER (Diakonikolas et al. 2019) - SVD-of-gradients outlier meta-algorithm
DUTI (Zhang et al. 2018) - Debugging Using Trusted Items, requires a small trusted clean validation set
